text_original: Qi of Yin's mother was Jian Di, who was one of the daughters of Yousong and the secondary wife of Emperor Ku.
text_normalized: Qi of Yin's mother was Jian Di, who was one of the daughters of Yousong and the secondary wife of Emperor Ku.

text_original: She was going with her two sisters to bathe, when she saw a dark bird drop its egg.
text_normalized: She was going with her two sisters to bathe, when she saw a dark bird drop its egg.

text_original: Jian Di picked it up, and swallowed it, and thus being with child gave birth to Qi.
text_normalized: Jian Di picked it up, and swallowed it, and thus being with child gave birth to Qi.

text_original: When Qi grew up, he was successful in assisting Yu to control the flood, and the Emperor Shun, directing Qi, said:
text_normalized: When Qi grew up, he was successful in assisting Yu to control the flood, and the Emperor Shun, directing Qi, said:

text_original: The people are wanting in affection for one another, and do not observe the five orders of relationship.
text_normalized: The people are wanting in affection for one another, and do not observe the five orders of relationship.

text_original: You, as Minister of Instruction, should reverently inculcate the lessons of duty belonging to those five orders, but do so with gentleness.
text_normalized: You, as Minister of Instruction, should reverently inculcate the lessons of duty belonging to those five orders, but do so with gentleness.

text_original: He held in fief the principality of Shang, and was given the Surname Of Zi.

text_original: Qi flourished in the reigns of Yao, Shun, and the great Yu.
text_normalized: Qi flourished in the reigns of Yao, Shun, and the great Yu.

text_original: His services were manifest to the people, who were accordingly at peace.

text_original: From Qi to Tang the Completer there were eight changes of the capital.
text_normalized: From Qi to Tang the Completer there were eight changes of the capital.

text_original: Tang at first dwelt in Bo, choosing the residence of the first king, and the 'Emperor's Announcement' was written.
text_normalized: Tang at first dwelt in Bo, choosing the residence of the first king, and the 'Emperor's Announcement' was written.

text_original: When Tang made an expedition against the princes, the chief of Ge was not offering the proper sacrifices, and Tang began by punishing him.

text_original: Tang said:

text_original: if he seeks to make an impression on the people, should he not know how to govern?'

text_original: Yiyin said:
text_normalized: Yiyin said:

text_original: 'That is clear!
text_normalized: 'That is clear!

text_original: If you say that you will listen to reason you may offer yourself as ruler of the State.
text_normalized: If you say that you will listen to reason you may offer yourself as ruler of the State.

text_original: Whether the people do right or not depends entirely on the king and his ministers.
text_normalized: Whether the people do right or not depends entirely on the king and his ministers.

text_original: Rouse yourself to action!' Tang said:
text_normalized: Rouse yourself to action!' Tang said:

text_original: 'If you cannot respect my commands, I will inflict upon you the extreme penalty death.

text_original: You will not obtain forgiveness.' The punitive expedition of Tang was written.

text_original: Yiyin's name was A-Heng.

text_normalized: A-Heng wanted to meet Tang, but had no opportunity of doing so

text_original: he therefore became cook to the prince of Xin, and while bringing Tang dishes to taste urged him to perfect himself in the way of the ancient kings.

text_original: Some say that when Yiyin was living in retirement Tang sent five times to invite him to a meeting before he would obey him, and talk about matters connected with the guileless king and the nine rulers.
text_normalized: Some say that when Yiyin was living in retirement Tang sent five times to invite him to a meeting before he would obey him, and talk about matters connected with the guileless king and the nine rulers.

text_original: Yiyin went from Tang to Xia.
text_normalized: Yiyin went from Tang to Xia.

text_original: Being indignant with the sovereign of Xia, he returned to Bo, and as he entered by the north gate met Rujiu and Rufang, and the 'Rujiu' and 'Rufang' were written.
text_normalized: Being indignant with the sovereign of Xia, he returned to Bo, and as he entered by the north gate met Rujiu and Rufang, and the 'Rujiu' and 'Rufang' were written.

text_original: Tang went out and saw a rustic, who was spreading nets in every direction, and vowing that every bird in the sky should go into his net.
text_normalized: Tang went out and saw a rustic, who was spreading nets in every direction, and vowing that every bird in the sky should go into his net.

text_original: Tang said, 'What!
text_normalized: Tang said, 'What!

text_original: all?' Then, taking away the nets on three sides, he vowed that those which wanted to go to the left should go left, and those which wanted to go right should go right, and that only those which were the victims of fate should be caught in the net.
text_normalized: all?' Then, taking away the nets on three sides, he vowed that those which wanted to go to the left should go left, and those which wanted to go right should go right, and that only those which were the victims of fate should be caught in the net.

text_original: The princes, hearing of it, said, 'Tang's kindness is extreme, and extends even to birds and beasts.' At this time Jie of Xia was oppressive, and his rule dissipated, and one of the princes Kunwu rebelled, so Tang, levying an army, put himself at the head of the princes.
text_normalized: The princes, hearing of it, said, 'Tang's kindness is extreme, and extends even to birds and beasts.' At this time Jie of Xia was oppressive, and his rule dissipated, and one of the princes Kunwu rebelled, so Tang, levying an army, put himself at the head of the princes.

text_original: Yiyin followed Tang, who, grasping a halberd, marched against Kunwu, and then attacked Jie.
text_normalized: Yiyin followed Tang, who, grasping a halberd, marched against Kunwu, and then attacked Jie.

text_original: Tang said:
text_normalized: Tang said:

text_original: Come, ye multitudes of the people, listen ye all to my words.
text_normalized: Come, ye multitudes of the people, listen ye all to my words.

text_original: It is not I, the little child, who dare to raise a rebellion.
text_normalized: It is not I, the little child, who dare to raise a rebellion.

text_original: The ruler of Xia has committed many crimes.
text_normalized: The ruler of Xia has committed many crimes.

text_original: I have indeed heard the words of you all, but the Xia ruler is an offender, and, as I fear the Supreme god, I dare not but punish him.
text_normalized: I have indeed heard the words of you all, but the Xia ruler is an offender, and, as I fear the Supreme god, I dare not but punish him.

text_original: Now, as the Xia ruler has committed many crimes, Heaven has charged me to destroy him.
text_normalized: Now, as the Xia ruler has committed many crimes, Heaven has charged me to destroy him.

text_original: Now, ye multitudes, you are saying, 'Our sovereign does not compassionate us
text_normalized: Now, ye multitudes, you are saying, 'Our sovereign does not compassionate us

text_original: he disregards our husbandry, and his government is a cruel one.' You say, 'As to his crimes, what remedy have we?' The king of Xia does nothing but exhaust his people's strength, and treat the kingdom of Xia oppressively.
text_normalized: he disregards our husbandry, and his government is a cruel one.' You say, 'As to his crimes, what remedy have we?' The king of Xia does nothing but exhaust his people's strength, and treat the kingdom of Xia oppressively.

text_original: His people have all become idle, and are not in harmony with him, saying, 'When will this sun set?
text_normalized: His people have all become idle, and are not in harmony with him, saying, 'When will this sun set?

text_original: We shall all perish together.' Such being the conduct of the sovereign of Xia, I must advance.
text_normalized: We shall all perish together.' Such being the conduct of the sovereign of Xia, I must advance.

text_original: If you help me, the one man, to carry out the punishment decreed by Heaven, and I will greatly reward you.
text_normalized: If you help me, the one man, to carry out the punishment decreed by Heaven, and I will greatly reward you.

text_original: On no account disbelieve me.
text_normalized: On no account disbelieve me.

text_original: I will not retract my words.
text_normalized: I will not retract my words.

text_original: If you do not carry out the words of my speech, I will put you and your children to death
text_normalized: If you do not carry out the words of my speech, I will put you and your children to death

text_original: you shall not be pardoned.
text_normalized: you shall not be pardoned.

text_original: This being announced to the army, the speech of Tang was written.
text_normalized: This being announced to the army, the speech of Tang was written.

text_original: Tang then said, 'I am very warlike'
text_normalized: Tang then said, 'I am very warlike'

text_original: and he was styled the 'warlike king.' Jie was defeated in the wilds of Yousong, and fled to Mingtiao.
text_normalized: and he was styled the 'warlike king.' Jie was defeated in the wilds of Yousong, and fled to Mingtiao.

text_original: The army of Xia being entirely defeated, Tang smote Sanzong, where he captured the precious jewels.
text_normalized: The army of Xia being entirely defeated, Tang smote Sanzong, where he captured the precious jewels.

text_original: Yibo and Zhongbo wrote the 'Statutes and Jewels.' When Tang had conquered Xia, he wished to remove the altars to the spirits of the land, but was unable to do so, and the 'Altar of Xia' was written.

text_original: Yiyin made a report, and the princes being satisfied, Tang ascended the Imperial throne, and tranquillized the country within the four seas.

text_original: When Tang returned he came to Taijuantao, and Zhonglei wrote his announcement.
text_normalized: When Tang returned he came to Taijuantao, and Zhonglei wrote his announcement.

text_original: Having made an end of the sovereignty of Xia, Tang returned to Bo and wrote the 'Announcement of Tang.' In the third month the king came himself to the eastern suburb and made the following announcement to the princes and nobles:
text_normalized: Having made an end of the sovereignty of Xia, Tang returned to Bo and wrote the 'Announcement of Tang.' In the third month the king came himself to the eastern suburb and made the following announcement to the princes and nobles:

text_original: 'If you do not perform meritorious service for the people and be diligent in your business, I shall inflict the extreme punishment of death.
text_normalized: 'If you do not perform meritorious service for the people and be diligent in your business, I shall inflict the extreme punishment of death.

text_original: Do not murmur against me.' He also said:

text_original: 'Formerly Yu and Gaoyao laboured long in distant regions.
text_normalized: 'Formerly Yu and Gaoyao laboured long in distant regions.

text_original: They performed meritorious service for the people, who dwelt in peace.
text_normalized: They performed meritorious service for the people, who dwelt in peace.

text_original: On the east there was the Great river, on the north the Ji, on the west the Yellow river, and on the south the Huai.
text_normalized: On the east there was the Great river, on the north the Ji, on the west the Yellow river, and on the south the Huai.

text_original: These four streams were kept within bounds, and the people dwelt in safety.
text_normalized: These four streams were kept within bounds, and the people dwelt in safety.

text_original: Prince Millet told them how to sow and cultivate the various kinds of grain.
text_normalized: Prince Millet told them how to sow and cultivate the various kinds of grain.

text_original: These three chiefs all performed meritorious service for the people, and were therefore ennobled.

text_original: The words of the former kings cannot but rouse you to action.

text_original: If you are unprincipled you shall not rule in the State.

text_original: 'Do not murmur against me.' Thus he directed the princes.

text_original: Yiyin wrote the book 'Both possessed pure Virtue,' and Jiudan wrote the 'Illustrious Abode.' Tang altered the day of the New Year, and changed the colour of the dresses, white being uniformly worn at State functions.

text_normalized: After the demise of Tang his eldest son, Taiding, died before he could come to the throne

text_original: and so Taiding's Younger Brother Waibing, that is Emperor Waibing, came to the throne.

text_original: Emperor Waibing reigned three years, and died, and His Brother Zhongren, that is Emperor Zhongren, came to the throne.

text_original: Emperor Zhongren reigned four years, and died.
text_normalized: Emperor Zhongren reigned four years, and died.

text_original: Yiyin then set Taiding's son, Taijia, on the throne.

text_original: He was Tang the Completer's eldest legitimate grandson.
text_normalized: He was Tang the Completer's eldest legitimate grandson.

text_original: This was Emperor Taijia.
text_normalized: This was Emperor Taijia.

text_original: In the first year of Emperor Taijia, Yiyin wrote the 'Instructions of Yi,' the 'Declaration of the Appointment of Heaven,' and the 'Deceased Sovereign.' After Emperor Taijia had been on the throne three years, he proved unintelligent and cruel.
text_normalized: In the first year of Emperor Taijia, Yiyin wrote the 'Instructions of Yi,' the 'Declaration of the Appointment of Heaven,' and the 'Deceased Sovereign.' After Emperor Taijia had been on the throne three years, he proved unintelligent and cruel.

text_original: He did not obey Tang's laws, and his conduct was disorderly, so Yiyin placed him In The Dryandra.
text_normalized: He did not obey Tang's laws, and his conduct was disorderly, so Yiyin placed him In The Dryandra.

text_original: For three years Yiyin administered the government, and as ruler of the State gave audience to the nobles.
text_normalized: For three years Yiyin administered the government, and as ruler of the State gave audience to the nobles.

text_original: After the Emperor Taijia had dwelt in the Dryandra palace for three years, he repented of his errors, blamed himself, and amended his ways.
text_normalized: After the Emperor Taijia had dwelt in the Dryandra palace for three years, he repented of his errors, blamed himself, and amended his ways.

text_original: Whereupon Yiyin met the Emperor Taijia and resigned to him the reins of government.
text_normalized: Whereupon Yiyin met the Emperor Taijia and resigned to him the reins of government.

text_original: Emperor Taijia became virtuous, the princes all returned to the Yin capital, and the people were tranquil.
text_normalized: Emperor Taijia became virtuous, the princes all returned to the Yin capital, and the people were tranquil.

text_original: Yiyin praised him, and wrote the 'Instructions to Taijia' in three books.
text_normalized: Yiyin praised him, and wrote the 'Instructions to Taijia' in three books.

text_original: Commending Emperor Taijia, he honoured him with the title of 'Great Master.' 'Great Master' died, and His Son Woding came to the throne.
text_normalized: Commending Emperor Taijia, he honoured him with the title of 'Great Master.' 'Great Master' died, and His Son Woding came to the throne.

text_normalized: When they had buried Yiyin at Bo, Jiu Dan set forth as lessons the doings of Yiyin

text_original: and the 'Woding' was written.

text_original: In his reign Yizhi became prime minister, when there were omens in Bo, for a mulberry tree and a stalk of grain grew up together in the court.
text_normalized: In his reign Yizhi became prime minister, when there were omens in Bo, for a mulberry tree and a stalk of grain grew up together in the court.

text_original: They attained full size in one evening, and Emperor Taiwu, being alarmed, questioned Yizhi on the subject.
text_normalized: They attained full size in one evening, and Emperor Taiwu, being alarmed, questioned Yizhi on the subject.

text_original: Yizhi said:
text_normalized: Yizhi said:

text_original: 'I, your servant, have heard that virtue is not overcome by evil omens.
text_normalized: 'I, your servant, have heard that virtue is not overcome by evil omens.

text_original: There may be defects in your Majesty's government, but let your Majesty cultivate virtue.' Taiwu followed his advice, and the ominous mulberry withered away.
text_normalized: There may be defects in your Majesty's government, but let your Majesty cultivate virtue.' Taiwu followed his advice, and the ominous mulberry withered away.

text_original: Yizhi consulted with Xian the wizard, who governed the king's household admirably, and wrote the 'Xian-ai' and the 'Taiwu.' Emperor Taiwu spoke on the subject with Yizhi in court, and said he was disloyal.
text_normalized: Yizhi consulted with Xian the wizard, who governed the king's household admirably, and wrote the 'Xian-ai' and the 'Taiwu.' Emperor Taiwu spoke on the subject with Yizhi in court, and said he was disloyal.

text_original: In his reign the Yins had their capital on the north of the Yellow river, but Pangeng crossed to the south of the river, and reoccupied the old palace of Tang the Completer.
text_normalized: In his reign the Yins had their capital on the north of the Yellow river, but Pangeng crossed to the south of the river, and reoccupied the old palace of Tang the Completer.

text_original: This made the fifth change of capital, and, as they had no fixed place of abode, the people of Yin murmured and repined, for they did not like moving about.
text_normalized: This made the fifth change of capital, and, as they had no fixed place of abode, the people of Yin murmured and repined, for they did not like moving about.

text_original: Pangeng made the following announcement to the princes and ministers:

text_original: 'Formerly our exalted sovereign Tang the Completer, in conjunction with your ancestors, decided what laws and regulations of the empire should be attended to or set aside, but if you do not make an effort how can you attain perfection?' He then crossed over to the south of the river, set up his capital at Bo, and having adopted Tang's system of government, the people were thereby tranquillized, and the fortunes of Yin were again in the ascendant.

text_original: Emperor Xiaoyi died, and his Son Emperor Wuding came to the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Xiaoyi died, and his Son Emperor Wuding came to the throne.

text_original: When Emperor Wuding was on the throne, he pondered how the Yin dynasty could be revivified, but as he had not obtained an assistant he did not speak for three years, government affairs having to be conducted by the prime minister, who examined into the customs of the country.
text_normalized: When Emperor Wuding was on the throne, he pondered how the Yin dynasty could be revivified, but as he had not obtained an assistant he did not speak for three years, government affairs having to be conducted by the prime minister, who examined into the customs of the country.

text_original: Wuding dreamed one night that he had found a holy man named Yue, and, in order that he might secure the man he had seen in his dream, he passed under review his officers and ministers of State, but not one of them was the right man.
text_normalized: Wuding dreamed one night that he had found a holy man named Yue, and, in order that he might secure the man he had seen in his dream, he passed under review his officers and ministers of State, but not one of them was the right man.

text_original: He then made all his officers search for him in the wilds, and Yue was discovered at the crag of Fu.
text_normalized: He then made all his officers search for him in the wilds, and Yue was discovered at the crag of Fu.

text_original: At this time Yue was a clerk, not a builder at the crag of Fu.
text_normalized: At this time Yue was a clerk, not a builder at the crag of Fu.

text_original: He had an audience of Wuding, who said, 'That is the right man.' Having talked with him, and finding that he really was a holy man, Wuding promoted him to be his prime minister.
text_normalized: He had an audience of Wuding, who said, 'That is the right man.' Having talked with him, and finding that he really was a holy man, Wuding promoted him to be his prime minister.

text_original: The kingdom of Yin was well governed in consequence, and he was named after the crag of Fu, being called Fu Yue.
text_normalized: The kingdom of Yin was well governed in consequence, and he was named after the crag of Fu, being called Fu Yue.

text_original: Wuding was sacrificing to Tang the Completer the next day, when a pheasant flew up, lighted on the ear of a tripod, and crowed.
text_normalized: Wuding was sacrificing to Tang the Completer the next day, when a pheasant flew up, lighted on the ear of a tripod, and crowed.

text_original: Wuding was alarmed, But Zuji said the king should not be anxious
text_normalized: Wuding was alarmed, But Zuji said the king should not be anxious

text_original: he must first rectify the administration of affairs.
text_normalized: he must first rectify the administration of affairs.

text_original: Zuji accordingly lectured the king, saying, 'In its superintendence over men below, Heaven pays special regard to their proper behaviour, and bestows on them accordingly length of years or the reverse.
text_normalized: Zuji accordingly lectured the king, saying, 'In its superintendence over men below, Heaven pays special regard to their proper behaviour, and bestows on them accordingly length of years or the reverse.

text_original: Heaven does not cut short men's lives
text_normalized: Heaven does not cut short men's lives

text_original: they bring them to an end themselves.
text_normalized: they bring them to an end themselves.

text_original: Some men may not have conformed to virtue, and will not acknowledge their crimes
text_normalized: Some men may not have conformed to virtue, and will not acknowledge their crimes

text_original: Heaven then charges them to correct their conduct, but they say, 'What shall we do?' Ah!
text_normalized: Heaven then charges them to correct their conduct, but they say, 'What shall we do?' Ah!

text_original: the king should continuously treat the people with respect.
text_normalized: the king should continuously treat the people with respect.

text_original: Are they not Heaven's descendants?
text_normalized: Are they not Heaven's descendants?

text_original: Be constant in sacrificing, and do not worship with the rites of a discarded religion.' Wuding instituted a government reform, and practised virtue.
text_normalized: Be constant in sacrificing, and do not worship with the rites of a discarded religion.' Wuding instituted a government reform, and practised virtue.

text_original: The whole nation rejoiced, and the fortunes of Yin again flourished.
text_normalized: The whole nation rejoiced, and the fortunes of Yin again flourished.

text_original: Emperor Wuding died, and his Son Emperor Zugeng came to the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Wuding died, and his Son Emperor Zugeng came to the throne.

text_original: Zuji commended Wuding for considering the omen of the pheasant as a ground for practising virtue, and conferred on him the posthumous title of 'exalted ancestor,' and the 'Day of the Supplementary Sacrifice of Gaozong' and the 'Instructions [to Gaozong]' were written.
text_normalized: Zuji commended Wuding for considering the omen of the pheasant as a ground for practising virtue, and conferred on him the posthumous title of 'exalted ancestor,' and the 'Day of the Supplementary Sacrifice of Gaozong' and the 'Instructions [to Gaozong]' were written.

text_original: Emperor Zugeng died, and His Brother Zujia, that is Emperor Jia, came to the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Zugeng died, and His Brother Zujia, that is Emperor Jia, came to the throne.

text_original: He was dissipated, and the fortunes of Yin again waned.
text_normalized: He was dissipated, and the fortunes of Yin again waned.

text_original: Emperor Jia died, and his Son Emperor Linxin sat on the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Jia died, and his Son Emperor Linxin sat on the throne.

text_original: Emperor Linxin died, and His Brother Gengding, that is Emperor Gengding, sat on the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Linxin died, and His Brother Gengding, that is Emperor Gengding, sat on the throne.

text_original: Emperor Gengding died, and his Son Emperor Wuyi sat on the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Gengding died, and his Son Emperor Wuyi sat on the throne.

text_original: The Yins again left Bo, and crossed to the north bank of the river.
text_normalized: The Yins again left Bo, and crossed to the north bank of the river.

text_original: The Emperor Wuyi was unprincipled and made images, which he called 'Heavenly gods.' With these he played chess, ordering some one to make the moves for them
text_normalized: The Emperor Wuyi was unprincipled and made images, which he called 'Heavenly gods.' With these he played chess, ordering some one to make the moves for them

text_original: and when the 'celestial gods' did not win he abused them, and making a leather bag, filled it with blood, threw it up and shot at it.
text_normalized: and when the 'celestial gods' did not win he abused them, and making a leather bag, filled it with blood, threw it up and shot at it.

text_original: This he called shooting at Heaven.
text_normalized: This he called shooting at Heaven.

text_original: While Wuyi was hunting between the Yellow and Wei rivers, there was a clap of thunder, and Wuyi was struck dead by lightning.
text_normalized: While Wuyi was hunting between the Yellow and Wei rivers, there was a clap of thunder, and Wuyi was struck dead by lightning.

text_original: His Son Emperor Taiding came to the throne.
text_normalized: His Son Emperor Taiding came to the throne.

text_original: Emperor Taiding died, and his Son Emperor Yi sat on the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Taiding died, and his Son Emperor Yi sat on the throne.

text_original: In his reign the fortunes of Yin declined still further.
text_normalized: In his reign the fortunes of Yin declined still further.

text_original: Emperor Yi'S eldest son was Qi, viscount of Wei.
text_normalized: Emperor Yi'S eldest son was Qi, viscount of Wei.

text_original: Qi's mother being of low caste, he could not be heir to the throne.
text_normalized: Qi's mother being of low caste, he could not be heir to the throne.

text_original: His younger Son Was Xin, whose mother was the principal consort, and so he became the heir-apparent.
text_normalized: His younger Son Was Xin, whose mother was the principal consort, and so he became the heir-apparent.

text_original: Emperor Yi died, and his son Xin sat on the throne.
text_normalized: Emperor Yi died, and his son Xin sat on the throne.

text_original: Emperor Xin was called by everybody in The Empire Zhou.
text_normalized: Emperor Xin was called by everybody in The Empire Zhou.

text_original: Emperor Zhou's discrimination was acute, his hearing and sight particularly good, his natural abilities extraordinary, and his physical strength equal to that of a wild beast.
text_normalized: Emperor Zhou's discrimination was acute, his hearing and sight particularly good, his natural abilities extraordinary, and his physical strength equal to that of a wild beast.

text_original: He had cunning enough to evade reproofs, and volubility enough to gloss over his faults.
text_normalized: He had cunning enough to evade reproofs, and volubility enough to gloss over his faults.

text_original: He boasted that he was above his ministers on the ground of ability, and that he surpassed the people of the empire on account of his reputation.
text_normalized: He boasted that he was above his ministers on the ground of ability, and that he surpassed the people of the empire on account of his reputation.

text_original: He indulged in wine, women, and lusts of all sorts.
text_normalized: He indulged in wine, women, and lusts of all sorts.

text_original: His Partiality For Taji caused him to carry out whatever she desired, so that his ministers had to devise new forms of dissipation, the most depraved dances and extravagant music
text_normalized: His Partiality For Taji caused him to carry out whatever she desired, so that his ministers had to devise new forms of dissipation, the most depraved dances and extravagant music

text_original: he increased the taxation in order to fill the Stag tower with money, and to store the granary at 'Big bridge.' He made a collection of dogs, horses, and curiosities, with which he filled his palaces
text_normalized: he increased the taxation in order to fill the Stag tower with money, and to store the granary at 'Big bridge.' He made a collection of dogs, horses, and curiosities, with which he filled his palaces

text_original: and enlarging his parks and towers at Shaqiu, procured numbers of wild beasts and birds and put them therein.
text_normalized: and enlarging his parks and towers at Shaqiu, procured numbers of wild beasts and birds and put them therein.

text_original: He slighted the spirits, assembled a great number of play actors at Shaqiu, made a pond of wine, hung the trees with meat, made men and women chase each other about quite naked, and had drinking bouts the whole night long.
text_normalized: He slighted the spirits, assembled a great number of play actors at Shaqiu, made a pond of wine, hung the trees with meat, made men and women chase each other about quite naked, and had drinking bouts the whole night long.

text_original: The people murmured, and when the nobles rebelled Zhouxin increased the severity of his punishments, instituting the punishment of roasting.
text_normalized: The people murmured, and when the nobles rebelled Zhouxin increased the severity of his punishments, instituting the punishment of roasting.

text_original: He appointed Chang Chief of the West, the prince of Jiu, and the prince of E his three principal ministers.
text_normalized: He appointed Chang Chief of the West, the prince of Jiu, and the prince of E his three principal ministers.

text_original: The prince of Jiu had a beloved daughter who was sent in to the emperor, and when she disapproved of his debaucheries the tyrant killed her in his rage, and made mincemeat of her father.
text_normalized: The prince of Jiu had a beloved daughter who was sent in to the emperor, and when she disapproved of his debaucheries the tyrant killed her in his rage, and made mincemeat of her father.

text_original: The prince of E objected, and vehemently remonstrated with him, whereupon he was sliced to pieces.
text_normalized: The prince of E objected, and vehemently remonstrated with him, whereupon he was sliced to pieces.

text_original: Chang Chief of the West, hearing of all this, sighed furtively, but 'Tiger' the prince of Chong, being aware of it, informed the tyrant, who thereupon cast Chief of the West into prison at Youli.
text_normalized: Chang Chief of the West, hearing of all this, sighed furtively, but 'Tiger' the prince of Chong, being aware of it, informed the tyrant, who thereupon cast Chief of the West into prison at Youli.

text_original: His servant Hongyao and others procured a pretty girl, rare curiosities, and fine horses, which they presented to the tyrant, who thereupon pardoned Chief of the West.
text_normalized: His servant Hongyao and others procured a pretty girl, rare curiosities, and fine horses, which they presented to the tyrant, who thereupon pardoned Chief of the West.

text_original: The latter went forth and gave the country to the west of the Luo river to the tyrant, and begged that he would abolish the punishment of roasting.
text_normalized: The latter went forth and gave the country to the west of the Luo river to the tyrant, and begged that he would abolish the punishment of roasting.

text_original: The emperor agreed to this, and gave him bows, arrows, axes, and halberds, with a commission to start on a warlike expedition.
text_normalized: The emperor agreed to this, and gave him bows, arrows, axes, and halberds, with a commission to start on a warlike expedition.

text_original: He was appointed Chief of the West, and Feizhong was employed in the government.
text_normalized: He was appointed Chief of the West, and Feizhong was employed in the government.

text_original: Feizhong was fond of flattery and greedy of gain, so the men of Yin were not attached to him.
text_normalized: Feizhong was fond of flattery and greedy of gain, so the men of Yin were not attached to him.

text_original: The tyrant also gave Elai an appointment, but Elai was fond of vilifying persons, so the princes became more and more estranged from the court.
text_normalized: The tyrant also gave Elai an appointment, but Elai was fond of vilifying persons, so the princes became more and more estranged from the court.

text_original: Now Chief of the West, on returning from his expedition, secretly cultivated virtue, and was charitable
text_normalized: Now Chief of the West, on returning from his expedition, secretly cultivated virtue, and was charitable

text_original: many of the princes revolted from the tyrant and gave their allegiance to Chief of the West, who from this time gained in influence, while the tyrant rather lost his authority.
text_normalized: many of the princes revolted from the tyrant and gave their allegiance to Chief of the West, who from this time gained in influence, while the tyrant rather lost his authority.

text_original: The monarch's son Bigan remonstrated with his father, but he was not listened to.
text_normalized: The monarch's son Bigan remonstrated with his father, but he was not listened to.

text_original: Shangrong praised his worth, and the people loved him, but the tyrant set him aside.
text_normalized: Shangrong praised his worth, and the people loved him, but the tyrant set him aside.

text_original: Chief of the West marched against and conquered the Ji State, and the tyrant's minister Zu-yi heard of it, and blaming [the house of] Zhou hurried off in alarm to report it to the tyrant.
text_normalized: Chief of the West marched against and conquered the Ji State, and the tyrant's minister Zu-yi heard of it, and blaming [the house of] Zhou hurried off in alarm to report it to the tyrant.

text_original: He said:
text_normalized: He said:

text_original: 'Heaven is bringing to an end the destiny of our dynasty of Yin
text_normalized: 'Heaven is bringing to an end the destiny of our dynasty of Yin

text_original: great men and the ancient tortoise do not venture to foretell good fortune.
text_normalized: great men and the ancient tortoise do not venture to foretell good fortune.

text_original: It is not that the former kings do not aid us men of this later time
text_normalized: It is not that the former kings do not aid us men of this later time

text_original: but you, O king, by your dissoluteness and oppression are cutting yourself off.
text_normalized: but you, O king, by your dissoluteness and oppression are cutting yourself off.

text_original: Heaven has therefore rejected us
text_normalized: Heaven has therefore rejected us

text_original: we do not eat our meals in peace, we do not consider our heavenly nature, we do not follow and observe the statutes.
text_normalized: we do not eat our meals in peace, we do not consider our heavenly nature, we do not follow and observe the statutes.

text_original: Our people are now all longing for the destruction of the dynasty, saying, Why does not Heaven send down its awe-inspiring authority?
text_normalized: Our people are now all longing for the destruction of the dynasty, saying, Why does not Heaven send down its awe-inspiring authority?

text_original: Why is not its great decree manifested?
text_normalized: Why is not its great decree manifested?

text_original: What remedy is there against the present king?' The tyrant said:
text_normalized: What remedy is there against the present king?' The tyrant said:

text_original: 'Is not my life secured by the decree of Heaven?' Zu-yi returned, and said, 'The tyrant cannot be remonstrated with.' Chief of the West having died, King Wu of Zhou in his march eastward arrived at the ford of Meng.
text_normalized: 'Is not my life secured by the decree of Heaven?' Zu-yi returned, and said, 'The tyrant cannot be remonstrated with.' Chief of the West having died, King Wu of Zhou in his march eastward arrived at the ford of Meng.

text_original: The princes revolted, and 800 princes of the house of Zhou having assembled declared that the tyrant ought to be attacked.
text_normalized: The princes revolted, and 800 princes of the house of Zhou having assembled declared that the tyrant ought to be attacked.

text_original: King Wu said, 'You know nothing of Heaven's decree,' and retired.
text_normalized: King Wu said, 'You know nothing of Heaven's decree,' and retired.

text_original: The tyrant abandoned himself all the more to lust and dissipation, and the viscount of Wei remonstrated with him several times, but he would not heed, so having consulted with the senior and junior tutors the viscount of Wei withdrew from court.
text_normalized: The tyrant abandoned himself all the more to lust and dissipation, and the viscount of Wei remonstrated with him several times, but he would not heed, so having consulted with the senior and junior tutors the viscount of Wei withdrew from court.

text_original: Bigan said, 'A minister cannot but argue to the death'
text_normalized: Bigan said, 'A minister cannot but argue to the death'

text_original: he accordingly remonstrated vehemently with the tyrant, who in a rage said, 'I have heard that the heart of a holy man has seven apertures,' and cut Bigan open to look at his heart.
text_normalized: he accordingly remonstrated vehemently with the tyrant, who in a rage said, 'I have heard that the heart of a holy man has seven apertures,' and cut Bigan open to look at his heart.

text_original: The viscount of Ji, in terror, then feigned himself mad, and became a slave, and the tyrant again imprisoned him.
text_normalized: The viscount of Ji, in terror, then feigned himself mad, and became a slave, and the tyrant again imprisoned him.

text_original: The senior and junior tutors of Yin, accordingly, taking the sacrificial and musical implements, hastened to the Zhou State, and King Wu of Zhou upon this marched at the head of the princes to attack the tyrant, who also sent out an army to withstand him in the plain of Mu.
text_normalized: The senior and junior tutors of Yin, accordingly, taking the sacrificial and musical implements, hastened to the Zhou State, and King Wu of Zhou upon this marched at the head of the princes to attack the tyrant, who also sent out an army to withstand him in the plain of Mu.

text_original: On the day Jiazi the tyrant's troops were beaten, and he himself fled to the Stag tower, which he ascended, and, putting on his gorgeous robes and jewels, burnt himself to death.
text_normalized: On the day Jiazi the tyrant's troops were beaten, and he himself fled to the Stag tower, which he ascended, and, putting on his gorgeous robes and jewels, burnt himself to death.

text_original: King Wu of Zhou then cut off the tyrant's head and exhibited it on a pole
text_normalized: King Wu of Zhou then cut off the tyrant's head and exhibited it on a pole

text_original: he also slew Daji, released the viscount of Ji from prison, raised a tumulus over the grave of Bigan, and made a eulogy to the memory of Shangrong.
text_normalized: he also slew Daji, released the viscount of Ji from prison, raised a tumulus over the grave of Bigan, and made a eulogy to the memory of Shangrong.

text_original: His sons, Wugeng and Lufu, were appointed to continue the sacrifices to the Yins.
text_normalized: His sons, Wugeng and Lufu, were appointed to continue the sacrifices to the Yins.

text_original: He restored Pangeng's mode of administration, and the people of Yin were greatly rejoiced.
text_normalized: He restored Pangeng's mode of administration, and the people of Yin were greatly rejoiced.

text_original: Whereupon King Wu of Zhou became Son Of Heaven.
text_normalized: Whereupon King Wu of Zhou became Son Of Heaven.

text_original: His descendants abolished the Title Of Di, and Called Themselves Kings
text_normalized: His descendants abolished the Title Of Di, and Called Themselves Kings

text_original: and the descendants of the Yins were made princes subordinate to the house of Zhou.
text_normalized: and the descendants of the Yins were made princes subordinate to the house of Zhou.

text_original: After the death of King Wu of Zhou, Wugeng, Guanshu, and Caishu rebelled.
text_normalized: After the death of King Wu of Zhou, Wugeng, Guanshu, and Caishu rebelled.

text_original: King Cheng ordered the duke of Zhou to execute them, and the viscount of Wei was established in the Song State To Continue a descendant of the Yins.
text_normalized: King Cheng ordered the duke of Zhou to execute them, and the viscount of Wei was established in the Song State To Continue a descendant of the Yins.
